rule: Use skeleton loaders that mirror the final layout. Never a bare spinner for content loading.
why: A spinner conveys 'something is happening'; a skeleton conveys 'what is coming and where', which removes layout shift and reduces perceived wait.
verify: grep for spinner/loader components; confirm each content-loading state renders a shaped placeholder, not a rotating icon

rule: Every non-obvious control (icon-only buttons, truncated text, abbreviations, disabled actions) carries a tooltip AND an accessible name.
why: An icon without a label is a guess. A disabled button without a reason is a dead end.
verify: every icon-only control has aria-label + tooltip; disabled controls explain why

rule: Enable smooth scrolling for in-page navigation, gated behind prefers-reduced-motion.
why: Abrupt jumps feel cheap; unconditional smooth scrolling is a vestibular hazard.
verify: scroll-behavior:smooth present AND disabled under @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)

rule: EVERY screen must be verified at 375 / 768 / 1024 / 1440 - not just the ones that changed.
why: Responsiveness regressions hide on the screens nobody re-checked. Partial coverage is how a broken mobile layout ships.
verify: render each route at all four widths; capture evidence per breakpoint; no horizontal overflow, no clipped controls, targets >= 44px

rule: Ship a real favicon set (plus apple-touch-icon and theme-color). ASK the user to supply the artwork rather than inventing one.
why: A default framework favicon signals unfinished. The brand mark is the user's to provide - guessing it is worse than asking.
verify: favicon links present in head and files resolve 200; if absent, the report must contain an explicit request to the user for artwork

rule: Choose the exact semantic element for its purpose (header/nav/main/article/section/aside/footer, button vs a, h1-h6 in order).
why: Semantics are the machine-readable architecture of the page - both crawlers and screen readers depend on them. A div soup is invisible to both.
verify: one h1 per page, no skipped heading levels, landmarks present, no clickable div where a button belongs

rule: Use HTML tables exclusively for tabular data, with th/scope/caption. NEVER use a table for page layout.
why: A layout table lies to assistive tech about the content's structure.
verify: every table has th + scope; no table used purely for positioning

rule: Bind an explicit label to every input via for/id. NEVER use a placeholder as the label.
why: Placeholders vanish on focus, fail contrast, and are skipped by some screen readers - and a real label enlarges the click target.
verify: each input has a matching label[for]; no input whose only description is a placeholder

rule: Design the failure state: explicit, friendly, specific error messages tied to the field. Never rely on default browser validation bubbles.
why: A form is only as good as its failure state. 'Invalid input' tells the user nothing about how to proceed.
verify: each validation path renders an associated (aria-describedby) message that names the problem and the fix

rule: Load only what the current screen needs - paginate, virtualise long lists, lazy-load below-the-fold media, code-split heavy routes.
why: Shipping the whole dataset to render ten rows is the most common self-inflicted performance wound.
verify: check payload vs rendered rows; confirm pagination/virtualisation on unbounded lists and lazy-loading on offscreen media

rule: Every route sets a unique title and meta description, plus canonical, Open Graph and structured data where applicable.
why: Duplicate or missing metadata is the cheapest SEO loss there is.
verify: each route emits a distinct title + description; canonical present; OG tags render

rule: Optimise for Generative Engine Optimization: explicit prose with clear subject-verb connections, low dependency hops, entity clarity, answers to conversational prompts, and unlinked brand mentions.
why: AI-generated summaries cite content they can parse and attribute. Clear entities and self-contained sentences get cited; clever ambiguity does not.
verify: key pages answer a plain question in the first paragraph; brand/entity named explicitly rather than by pronoun; structured data present

rule: Private, internal or personal projects must ship noindex (robots meta + robots.txt) and must not be sitemapped.
why: A private tool indexed by a search engine is a data-exposure incident, not an SEO problem.
verify: robots meta noindex present on every route and robots.txt disallows; verify on the deployed host, not just in source

rule: No hidden N+1 queries - eager-load or batch. Inspect the query log, not just the code.
why: An N+1 looks fine in review and melts under real row counts; it is the classic latent scaling cliff.
verify: enable query logging on a representative request; assert query count does not grow with row count

rule: Connection pool must be bounded, leak-free, and recycle dead connections (timeouts, keepalive, retry on stale).
why: A leaked or dead connection surfaces as an intermittent production failure nobody can reproduce.
verify: pool max/idle/timeout configured; every acquire has a release path; stale-connection handling present

rule: Apply appropriate caching (HTTP cache headers, ETags, query/result cache, CDN) WITH an explicit invalidation story.
why: Caching without an invalidation plan trades a slow bug for a wrong one.
verify: cacheable responses set headers; each cache has a documented invalidation trigger

rule: Every public or unauthenticated endpoint is rate limited, keyed sensibly, with a 429 and Retry-After.
why: An unmetered public endpoint is a free DoS and a free credential-stuffing surface.
verify: hit an endpoint past the limit; confirm 429 + Retry-After and that the key cannot be trivially rotated

rule: Errors must be actionable: what failed, why, what to do next, and a correlation id. No bare 500s or leaked stack traces.
why: An unactionable error costs a support cycle; a leaked stack trace costs a security finding.
verify: trigger each error path; response names the problem and carries a correlation id; no internals leaked

rule: No secret may exist in committed code. Secrets live in .env (gitignored) or a secret manager; ship .env.example with empty keys.
why: A committed secret is not fixed by deleting it - it lives in git history forever and must be rotated.
verify: scan tree AND full git history; confirm .env is gitignored and .env.example holds no values

rule: Any commercial or data-collecting project ships a reachable privacy policy (and cookie notice where applicable), linked from the footer.
why: It is a legal requirement in most jurisdictions the moment you collect anything, including analytics.
verify: /privacy route resolves; linked from global footer; names what is collected and third parties involved

rule: Ship a real logging/observability framework - structured logs with levels and correlation ids, error tracking, and basic metrics. Never console.log as production logging.
why: If you cannot see a failure, you cannot operate the system; unstructured logs are unqueryable at the moment you need them.
verify: structured logger configured with levels; correlation id propagates across a request; errors reach a tracker

rule: Review dependencies each run: update what is outdated (respecting semver risk) and remove what is unused.
why: Dependency debt compounds silently until an urgent security patch forces a risky bulk upgrade.
verify: run the ecosystem outdated/audit tool and the unused-dependency check; both reported with a decision per item

rule: Enable HTTP/3 (QUIC) at the edge alongside HTTP/2 fallback.
why: Fewer round trips and better recovery on lossy mobile networks, for a config change.
verify: curl --http3 against the deployed host succeeds; Alt-Svc advertised

rule: Apply HSTS (with sensible max-age), a modern cipher suite, TLS 1.3, and OCSP stapling.
why: Downgrade and stripping attacks are cheap to prevent at the edge and expensive to explain afterwards.
verify: verify Strict-Transport-Security header, negotiated TLS version/cipher, and stapled OCSP on the deployed host

rule: Any user-facing AI feature enforces a PER-USER token/cost cap, not only a global budget - checked in a pre-call gate.
why: A global cap only decides who gets to exhaust it first; one user can spend the whole month's budget.
verify: locate the pre-call gate keyed by user; exceed it in a test and confirm refusal before the model call

rule: Stream tokens to the user for any interactive AI response, with a visible stop control and graceful mid-stream error handling.
why: Waiting on a complete generation feels broken; streaming makes latency legible and interruptible.
verify: observe incremental delivery over the wire; stop control aborts the request server-side, not just the UI

rule: Comment the non-obvious: why a decision was made, invariants, units, and gotchas. Not a restatement of the code.
why: The next developer inherits the reasoning, not just the syntax. A comment explaining 'why' survives a refactor that deletes the 'what'.
verify: non-obvious branches carry a rationale; no comment merely narrates the line below it

rule: SSH is key-only: PermitRootLogin no, PasswordAuthentication no, PermitEmptyPasswords no, modern key algorithms.
why: A password-authenticating SSH endpoint is brute-forced continuously by background scanners; key-only removes the entire class.
verify: sshd -T on the host shows the three directives; verify from a second session BEFORE trusting a change

rule: Firewall defaults to deny-inbound, allows only intended ports, and survives reboot.
why: An open-by-default host exposes every service you forgot was listening; a rule set that vanishes on reboot is worse than none because it is believed.
verify: ufw/nft/iptables shows default deny + explicit allows; confirm the ruleset is persisted and reload-safe

rule: Databases, caches and queues must never be reachable from the public internet - bind to localhost or a private interface.
why: A datastore bound to 0.0.0.0 is the single most common catastrophic server misconfiguration.
verify: ss -tulpn shows no 0.0.0.0 bind for db/cache/queue ports; confirm externally that the port does not answer

rule: Backups must exist, be current, be stored OFF the box, and have a restore that has actually been TESTED.
why: An untested backup is a hypothesis. Most backup failures are discovered during the first real restore, which is the worst possible time.
verify: show the most recent artefact and its age, prove it lives off-box, and evidence a restore test; absence of a restore test is itself the finding

rule: Automatic security updates are enabled AND demonstrably applying; a pending reboot after a kernel patch is tracked to completion.
why: A patched-but-not-rebooted kernel is an unpatched kernel; automation that silently stopped is worse than no automation.
verify: unattended-upgrades enabled and its log shows recent activity; reboot-required state is reported and scheduled

rule: Public SSH (and other exposed auth endpoints) sit behind fail2ban or equivalent throttling.
why: Rate-limiting authentication turns a continuous brute-force into a non-event.
verify: fail2ban-client status shows active jails covering the exposed services

rule: Services and containers run as a non-root user unless they provably need root; no unnecessary NOPASSWD sudo grants.
why: Root-by-default converts any application bug into a host compromise.
verify: container User is set and non-root; sudoers grants are enumerated and each justified

rule: Log rotation is configured and effective; no unbounded log growth, and journald has a size cap.
why: Unrotated logs are the most common cause of a full disk, which takes down every service at once.
verify: no log file over a sane threshold; logrotate/journald limits present and applied

rule: System time is synchronised (NTP/chrony) and the timezone is intentional.
why: Clock skew corrupts log correlation and silently breaks TLS validation and token expiry.
verify: timedatectl reports the clock synchronised and an NTP service active

rule: Flag code that can be made shorter with identical behaviour - remove needless intermediates, collapse duplicated branches, replace hand-rolled logic with a platform/std primitive - only when NO functionality, edge case, or readability is lost.
why: The smallest correct code is the cheapest to maintain and the hardest to hide a bug in. But 'clever and short' that drops an edge case is a regression, not a cleanup.
verify: for each proposed simplification, name the behaviour preserved and the lines removed; prove equivalence (tests still pass / same inputs to same outputs), never trade a handled edge case for brevity
